metadata.description: A curated knowledge base on cyber hygiene practices, threats, and source-grounded recommendations. The knowledge base supports a knowledge graph-grounded conversational agent and enables evaluation across validity, traceability, and stability.
curation_notes: Some threat mappings are initially inferred from Karayel et al.'s cyber hygiene categories and should later be supported by additional sources.
Page paragraph numbers are counted visually from the top-left of the page to the bottom-right of the page, regardless of section boundaries.

sources.title: Human factors in remote work: examining cyber hygiene practices
sources.notes: Primary source for cyber hygiene practices among remote workers and for the five cyber hygiene categories used as the initial taxonomy of the knowledge base.

sources.title: Cyber hygiene: The concept, its measure, and its initial tests
sources.notes: Original source for the Cyber Hygiene Inventory and its five cyber hygiene dimensions.

sources.title: An exploratory study of cyber hygiene behaviors and knowledge
sources.venue: Journal of Information Security and Applications
sources.notes: Survey and literature review that highlights user behavior in cyber hygiene and also defines threats such as phishing. They also talk about best practices.

sources.title: Cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavioral practices of university students
sources.venue: Information Security Journal: A Global Perspective
sources.notes: Peer-reviewed study on cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavioral practices among university students. Useful for strengthening knowledge-awareness-behavior KUs and findings about student cyber hygiene.

sources.title: A Survey on Ransomware: Evolution, Taxonomy, and Defense Solutions
sources.notes: Comprehensive survey of ransomware evolution, taxonomy, attack phases, target platforms, infection vectors, defense research, detection, and recovery mechanisms across PCs/workstations, mobile devices, and IoT/CPS platforms.

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: General cyber hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: General concepts and foundational definitions related to cyber hygiene.

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Storage and device hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Security measures and behaviors related to the configuration settings of digital devices.
examples: Conducting virus scans on storage devices
Keeping virus protection and device software up to date

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Transmission hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors when connecting to unknown or insecure wireless networks.
examples: Checking the quality of SSL certificates
Checking the lock icon in the web browser

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Social media hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors during the general use of social media.
examples: Evaluating the authenticity of friend requests
Being aware of who you are friends with on social media
Preventing location information from leaking in posts

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Authentication and credential hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors related to the use of passwords and authentication.

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Email and messaging hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Computer security behaviors related to email usage and messaging.
examples: Checking the subject of an incoming email
Checking the sender's email domain name
Checking spelling and grammar of email requests

threats.name: Malware infection
threats.definition: A compromise in which malicious software infects a device or storage medium.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. (2018) mentioned malware as a common cyber-crime, but the specific infection framing should be supported by an additional source.

threats.name: Data loss
threats.definition: Loss of access to important files or information due to device failure, accidental deletion, malware, or other incidents. Oz et al. explain that cryptographic ransomware encrypts victim files and can delete or overwrite original files, restricting access until ransom payment.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. directly mention backing up important files and secure data storage. Oz et al. explain that cryptographic ransomware encrypts victim files and can delete or overwrite original files, restricting access until ransom payment.

threats.name: Ransomware
threats.definition: Ransomware is a subset of malware that prevents or limits users from accessing their system and/or data until a ransom is paid.
threats.support_notes: Oz et al. directly define ransomware as malware that restricts access to a system or data until a ransom is paid. They distinguish cryptographic ransomware, which encrypts victim files, from locker ransomware, which prevents access to the system.

threats.name: Unpatched software
threats.definition: Risk arising from outdated software that may contain unresolved security vulnerabilities. It could lead to successful malware attacks such as ransomware by exploiting those security vulnerabilities.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. directly mention keeping device software up to date, but an additional source should support the specific vulnerability-risk framing. Oz et al. state that ransomware can employ vulnerabilities in system software or other applications, and that delayed upgrades or critical software patches contribute to ransomware success.

Karayel et al. directly link transmission hygiene to unknown or insecure wireless networks and websites without SSL security certificates. Cain et al. state that using unsecured Wi-Fi hotspots can compromise a network. Baraković and Baraković Husić report that many students use public Wi-Fi networks and show uncertainty about password-protected Wi-Fi safety and default Wi-Fi traffic encryption.

threats.name: Man-in-the-middle attack
threats.definition: An attack in which a third party intercepts or manipulates communication between two parties.
threats.support_notes: Inferred from SSL certificate and insecure connection practices. Requires an additional source for a direct man-in-the-middle definition.

threats.name: Social engineering
threats.definition: Manipulation of users into revealing information or performing unsafe actions.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. explicitly mention social engineering frauds in relation to email and messaging hygiene in remote work.

threats.name: Impersonation
threats.definition: A deception tactic in which an attacker pretends to be a trusted person, organization, or account.
threats.support_notes: Supported by practices such as evaluating authenticity of social media friend requests and checking sender email domains. Additional sources may strengthen the explicit impersonation framing.

threats.name: Oversharing personal information
threats.definition: Exposure of personal or sensitive information through excessive or poorly controlled sharing. Users may freely share passwords or private information over social networks.
threats.support_notes: Supported by Karayel et al.'s social media hygiene examples, including awareness of who users are friends with and managing privacy settings. Cain et al. (2018) note that users may freely share passwords or private information on social networks, as attackers focus on stealing that information.

threats.name: Privacy exposure
threats.definition: Risk of unintended disclosure of personal information due to weak privacy settings or unsafe online behavior.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. directly include managing privacy settings within social media hygiene. Cain et al. report participant behavior involving real name and date-of-birth disclosure on social media sites.

threats.name: Credential targeting
threats.definition: Attempts by attackers to target user information, usernames, passwords, or authentication mechanisms.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. discuss authentication and credential hygiene and state that user information and passwords are frequently targeted by hackers. Cain et al. state that attackers may seek user information and that phishing can release usernames and passwords.

threats.name: Credential theft
threats.definition: Unauthorized acquisition or misuse of usernames, passwords, or other authentication information.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. discuss attackers stealing user information and phishing scams releasing usernames and passwords. The specific credential-theft label is a knowledge-base mapping.

threats.name: Brute-force credential attack
threats.definition: An attempt to guess credentials through repeated login attempts.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. state that users are advised to select strong passwords to prevent guessing attacks. The broader brute-force framing remains a terminology mapping.

threats.name: Unauthorized access
threats.definition: Access to an account, device, or system without legitimate permission.
threats.support_notes: Inferred from authentication practices such as changing defaults and enabling MFA. Requires an additional source for a direct unauthorized-access definition.

threats.name: Phishing
threats.definition: A deceptive attack in which users are tricked into revealing information, clicking malicious links, opening harmful attachments, or performing unsafe actions.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. explicitly mention phishing attacks in relation to email and messaging hygiene. Cain et al. (2018) also mention phishing as a common cyber-crime.

threats.name: Email scam and e-fraud
threats.definition: A fraudulent email-based attempt to deceive users into unsafe actions or disclosure of information.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. mention e-fraud in relation to email and messaging hygiene.

threats.name: User noncompliance with security practices
threats.definition: A behavioral cybersecurity risk in which users fail to follow recommended or required security practices, increasing the likelihood of security incidents.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. discuss human factors as a weak link in security systems and state that noncompliance with security practices is a primary cause of security breaches.

threats.name: Insufficient organizational cybersecurity measures
threats.definition: A contextual cybersecurity risk in which weak or insufficient organizational security measures may be associated with poorer employee cyber hygiene practices.
threats.support_notes: Karayel et al. report a statistically significant positive relationship between organizational cybersecurity measures and employees' cyber hygiene practices.

threats.name: Web-based attack
threats.definition: A cyberattack category involving attacks delivered through or targeting web-based systems, websites, browsers, or online services.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. (2018) mention web-based attacks as one attack category experienced by organizations in the cited Ponemon cybercrime cost study. The source mentions the category but does not provide a detailed definition.

threats.name: Malicious code
threats.definition: Software code intended to harm, compromise, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access to systems or data.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. (2018) mention malicious code as one attack category experienced by organizations in the cited Ponemon cybercrime cost study. The source mentions the category but does not provide a detailed definition.

threats.name: Botnet
threats.definition: A network of compromised devices that can be controlled by an attacker to carry out malicious activity.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. (2018) mention botnets as one attack category experienced by organizations in the cited Ponemon cybercrime cost study. The source mentions the category but does not provide a detailed definition.

threats.name: Stolen device
threats.definition: A physical device theft incident that can expose systems, accounts, or information stored on the device.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. (2018) mention attacks related to stolen devices as one attack category experienced by organizations in the cited Ponemon cybercrime cost study. The source mentions the category but does not provide a detailed definition.

threats.name: Denial-of-service attack
threats.definition: An attack intended to disrupt or prevent normal access to a service, system, or network.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. (2018) mention denial-of-service attacks as one attack category experienced by organizations in the cited Ponemon cybercrime cost study. The source mentions the category but does not provide a detailed definition.

threats.name: Malicious insider
threats.definition: A threat involving harmful or unauthorized actions by someone with insider access to an organization, system, or data.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. (2018) mention malicious insiders as one attack category experienced by organizations in the cited Ponemon cybercrime cost study. The source mentions the category but does not provide a detailed definition.

threats.name: Infected USB drive
threats.definition: A removable storage device that may carry malicious software or otherwise compromise a system or network when used.
threats.support_notes: Cain et al. state that using infected USB drives can compromise a network and can lead to problems such as malware download.

practices.name: Conduct virus scans on storage devices
practices.description: Users should conduct virus scans on storage devices to detect potentially malicious or infected files.

practices.name: Back up important files
practices.description: Users should back up important files to reduce the impact of data loss, malware, or device failure. Oz et al. describe file recovery via cloud backup as one category of ransomware recovery research, supporting backups as part of ransomware recovery planning.

practices.name: Keep protection software up to date
practices.description: Users should keep antivirus protection and device software up to date.

practices.name: Check SSL certificate quality
practices.description: Users should check whether a website uses a valid SSL certificate before entering sensitive information.

practices.name: Check browser lock icon
practices.description: Users should check for the browser lock icon to identify whether a connection appears secured.

practices.name: Evaluate authenticity of friend requests
practices.description: Users should evaluate whether social media friend requests are authentic before accepting them.

practices.name: Manage privacy settings
practices.description: Users should manage social media privacy settings to control who can view their information.

practices.name: Change default usernames and passwords
practices.description: Users should change default usernames and passwords to reduce the risk of unauthorized access.

practices.name: Create complex usernames and passwords
practices.description: Users should create complex usernames and passwords to reduce the risk of credential compromise.

practices.name: Enable multifactor authentication
practices.description: Users should enable multifactor authentication to add an additional layer of account security.

practices.name: Check incoming email subjects
practices.description: Users should inspect the subject of incoming emails for suspicious, unexpected, or urgency-based requests that may indicate phishing or e-fraud.

practices.name: Check sender email domain
practices.description: Users should check the sender's email domain name before trusting an email request, especially when the message asks for sensitive information or urgent action.

practices.name: Check incoming email headers
practices.description: Users should check an incoming email's header as part of email and messaging hygiene.

practices.name: Check spelling and grammar of email requests
practices.description: Users should inspect spelling and grammar in email requests as possible warning signs of suspicious, fraudulent, or socially engineered messages.

practices.name: Address individual user behavior
practices.description: Cybersecurity guidance should address individual user behavior in addition to technical security measures.

practices.name: Use strong passwords
practices.description: Users should use strong passwords as part of routine cyber hygiene behavior.

practices.name: Change passwords regularly
practices.description: Users should change passwords regularly as part of maintaining cyber hygiene.

practices.name: Avoid password reuse
practices.description: Users should avoid reusing passwords across accounts to reduce credential-related risk.

practices.name: Update antivirus software
practices.description: Users should keep antivirus software up to date to reduce exposure to cyber threats.

practices.name: Securely store online information
practices.description: Users should securely store online information to protect personal and sensitive data.

practices.name: Conduct appropriate security scans
practices.description: Users should conduct appropriate security scans to detect cyber threats or attack attempts.

practices.name: Strengthen organizational cybersecurity measures
practices.description: Organizations should strengthen cybersecurity measures that support employees' cyber hygiene practices.

practices.name: Provide cybersecurity training
practices.description: Organizations should educate and train employees on cyber hygiene to support improved cyber hygiene behavior.

practices.name: Provide tailored cybersecurity training
practices.description: Organizations should tailor cyber hygiene improvement initiatives and training to employees' characteristics, such as educational level, age, or comprehension needs.

practices.name: Develop cybersecurity policies and procedures
practices.description: Organizations should develop cybersecurity policies and procedures that support remote work systems and foster a strong cyber hygiene culture.

practices.name: Provide incident management training
practices.description: Organizations should adopt incident management training as part of advanced cybersecurity measures for coordinating and managing incidents.

practices.name: Use cybersecurity protocols for incident coordination
practices.description: Organizations should adopt cybersecurity protocols for coordinating and managing cybersecurity incidents.

practices.name: Use a VPN on free or public Wi-Fi
practices.description: Users should use a VPN when using free or public Wi-Fi.

practices.name: Store logins and passwords in an encrypted online password vault
practices.description: Users should store logins and passwords in encrypted online password vaults.

practices.name: Set online alerts for personal information
practices.description: Users should place online alerts for their name or personal information.

practices.name: Create new and unique logins and passwords
practices.description: Users should create new and unique logins and passwords for each account.

practices.name: Manage browser-stored passwords
practices.description: Users should manage passwords stored by browsers as part of authentication and credential hygiene.

practices.name: Know social media connections
practices.description: Users should be aware of who they are connected to on social media.

practices.name: Reassess social media friends and connections
practices.description: Users should periodically reassess their social media friends and connections.

practices.name: Prevent location leaks in social media posts
practices.description: Users should ensure that their location information is not leaked in social media posts.

practices.name: Enable firewalls
practices.description: Users should enable firewalls on computing devices as part of storage and device hygiene.

practices.name: Check device operating system and software updates
practices.description: Users should check whether their device has the latest operating system, software update, or patch. Oz et al. identify delayed upgrades or critical software patches as a major success factor for ransomware and state that it is vital not to delay upgrades or security-related software patches.

practices.name: Monitor device resource and network usage
practices.description: Users should monitor device processes such as CPU, power, and network usage.

practices.name: Check email hyperlinks and attachments from unknown senders
practices.description: Users should check hyperlinks or attachments in emails from unknown senders as part of email and messaging hygiene. Oz et al. identify malicious emails as a common ransomware infection vector and note that ransomware may arrive as an attached malicious file or through a malicious link.

practices.name: Limit sharing personal information online
practices.description: Users should limit sharing private or personal information online, especially on social networks, including information such as real names, dates of birth, addresses, phone numbers, or email addresses where disclosure is unnecessary.

practices.name: Password protect mobile devices
practices.description: Users should protect mobile devices with authentication, such as a PIN password, as part of cyber hygiene.

practices.name: Secure wireless networks
practices.description: Users should secure their wireless networks, including password-protecting Wi-Fi, as part of cyber hygiene.

practices.name: Use checked and safe download links
practices.description: Users should prefer checked and safe links when downloading software or applications on computers or smartphones.

knowledge_units.title: Cyber hygiene as routine security behavior for protecting personal information
knowledge_units.claim: Cyber hygiene refers to routine cybersecurity practices that online users adopt to protect the security and integrity of their personal information on internet-connected devices against cyber threats.
knowledge_units.explanation: Cyber hygiene consists of regular security behaviors and habits that help protect internet-enabled devices and the safety and integrity of personal information from cyber threats.
recommended_actions: Adopt routine security habits when using internet-connected devices.
Protect personal, financial, and social information during online activity.
Follow security guidelines that reduce exposure to cyber threats.
Maintain secure behavior across devices, accounts, networks, and online communication.

knowledge_units.title: Authentication hygiene includes changing defaults, creating complex credentials, and enabling MFA
knowledge_units.claim: Authentication and credential hygiene involves changing default usernames and passwords, creating complex or unique credentials, managing browser-stored passwords, and enabling multifactor authentication.
knowledge_units.explanation: These practices describe authentication and credential hygiene behaviors that help users manage access to accounts and systems through usernames, passwords, stored credentials, and authentication mechanisms. Karayel et al. support changing defaults, creating complex credentials, and enabling multifactor authentication, while Vishwanath et al.'s Cyber Hygiene Inventory additionally includes creating new and unique logins and passwords and managing browser-stored passwords.
recommended_actions: Change default usernames and passwords.
Create complex usernames and passwords.
Create new and unique logins and passwords for each account.
Manage passwords stored by browsers.
Enable two-factor or multifactor authentication where possible.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Changing default usernames and passwords, creating complex usernames and passwords and enabling multifactor authentication

High authentication and credential hygiene, which involve using complex usernames and passwords, changing defaults and enabling multifactor authentication, are observed in the IT, defense, automotive and manufacturing industries. In contrast, the food, service and construction industries demonstrate the lowest levels. As user information and passwords are frequently targeted by hackers, maintaining high cyber hygiene is crucial for all internet users.
Changing default username from administrator to something unique on all Internet enabled devices

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Authentication and credential hygiene describe activities related to use of secure browsers, changing of default username and passwords, managing how browser stores passwords, etc.
It was found that lax security behavior included password reuse, lack of knowledge about the (in)secure passwords, sharing or writing down the passwords.

Furthermore, Florêncio et al. (2007) found out that strong passwords did not protect the online users from password stealing attacks, such as keylogging and phishing. Weak passwords invited brute-force attack.
Moreover, multi-factor authentication has appeared as an alternative practice to enhance security by requesting the user to provide more than one authentication factor contrary to only a password.

knowledge_units.claim: Routine cyber hygiene practices include checking devices for cyber threats, using strong passwords, changing passwords regularly, avoiding password reuse, updating antivirus software, securely storing online information, and conducting security scans.
knowledge_units.explanation: These practices represent recurring security habits that help users protect personal information, devices, and accounts from cyber threats. They complement the five cyber hygiene categories by providing concrete examples of day-to-day protective behavior.

Use strong passwords.

Avoid reusing passwords across accounts.
Keep antivirus software up to date.

Conduct appropriate security scans.

knowledge_units.title: Email and messaging hygiene helps users detect phishing, e-fraud, and social engineering attempts
knowledge_units.claim: Email and messaging hygiene involves checking email subjects, incoming email headers, sender domains, hyperlinks or attachments from unknown senders, and the spelling and grammar of email requests. knowledge_units.explanation: Email and messaging hygiene includes checking sender domains, email headers, links, attachments, and grammatical or typographical errors. Baraković and Baraković Husić also connect these practices to phishing avoidance and cite prior work suggesting that both conceptual and procedural knowledge can improve phishing threat avoidance behavior.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Transmission and browsing hygiene is associated with internet connections, since users are unaware of the risks related to connecting to an unknown or unsecure wireless-fidelity (Wi-Fi) network or leaving Wi-Fi turned on the whole time.

Good practices include using virtual private network (VPN) while using a public Wi-Fi network, checking the quality of the secure socket layer (SSL) certificate, and checking the lock icon on the web browser.

Results show that 60.3% of participants use public Wi-Fi networks that are available and free, 49.1% use password protected public Wi-Fi networks, and 37.7% use Wi-Fi networks that are familiar to them.

In terms of security awareness, it is concerning that only half of participants stated that it is not safe to use Wi-Fi networks which require access password for sensitive activities (Q15). The big issue is that almost 41% of participants are not sure.

Namely, two thirds of participants stated that they are not sure (59.3%) whether all Wi-Fi traffic is encrypted by default on all wireless routers (Q16) and that it is (24.4%). Only 16.3% answered correctly, that it is not encrypted.

knowledge_units.title: Storage and device hygiene includes backups, antivirus updates, secure storage, and virus scans
knowledge_units.claim: Storage and device hygiene involves conducting virus scans, backing up important files, keeping virus protection and device software up to date, enabling firewalls, checking device updates, monitoring device usage, securely storing data, and using checked or safe download links for software and applications.
knowledge_units.explanation: Karayel et al. describe storage and device hygiene as cyber hygiene behavior related to the configuration and security of digital devices, including scanning storage devices, backing up important files, keeping protection software updated, and securely storing data. Vishwanath et al.'s Cyber Hygiene Inventory additionally includes enabling firewalls, checking for operating system or software updates, keeping virus protection updated, and monitoring CPU, power, or network usage. Baraković and Baraković Husić add student-behavior evidence showing that scanning downloaded files and used devices is common among surveyed students, while backup behavior and download-link safety remain concerning. Oz et al. strengthen the importance of backups and updates in the ransomware context by describing backup-based recovery mechanisms and identifying delayed upgrades or critical software patches as a ransomware success factor.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: The main aim of this paper is to evaluate, analyze, and understand the level of cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavioral practices of university students and their mutual relations.
Results show that students have acceptable cyber hygiene behavior, but their awareness is not satisfactory, and their knowledge is quite low.

Also, the study shows the existence of some relations between gender and current education level and cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavior, as well as mutual interplay and relations between those cyber hygiene outcomes.
It is common view that user cyber hygiene can be measured by three user-oriented factors, i.e., knowledge, awareness, and behavior.
Knowledge and awareness refer to cognitive factors of knowledge and understanding, respectively, whereas behavioral practices are related to factor of use.
Good cyber hygiene is based on these three factors: when educated users are aware of what must be done, they implement it when necessary.

Tables 5–7 show the summaries of Chi-square test results related to relations of gender and current education level and cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavior organized by cyber hygiene dimensions.
Tables 8–10 cover results related to mutual interplay and relations between cyber hygiene knowledge, awareness, and behavioral practices obtained by conducting Chi-square test as well.

Research results show that university students in Bosnia and Herzegovina have relatively acceptable cyber hygiene behavior, but their awareness of cyber hygiene is not satisfactory, as well as their knowledge, which is quite low.

The results of Chi-square test are given in Table 8. The results show the statistically significant association between computer sharing behavioral practices and awareness to enable security software on those devices and to use safe links on smartphones. Smartphone usage habits have statistically significant relation to awareness of smartphone software updating. Wi-Fi usage habits have statistically significant association with awareness on using Wi-Fi for mobile banking.

Similar conclusion can be made regarding the relation between cyber hygiene behavioral practices and knowledge. This relation exists in some cases, but it needs to be examined in more detail in a focused study.

Data collected under items Q3, Q6, and Q12 has not been analyzed due to the same reasons stated above. We have analyzed the existence of relation between computer (Q4) and smartphone (Q5) sharing habits and cyber hygiene knowledge items (Q11, Q16-Q19, Q21-26). Then, we have analyzed the relation between password creation and management behavior (Q9 and Q10) and relevant knowledge data (Q11), and Wi-Fi usage data (Q13 and Q14) and relevant knowledge data (Q16).
Similar, after the analysis conducted by using Chi-square test with cyber hygiene awareness and knowledge items, one can conclude that the results indicate the partial existence of the relation.

There are statistically significant associations between awareness on Wi-Fi safety and correct answer on Q16-Q19 and Q23-24. Also, there are statistically significant associations between awareness on device and file checking and correct answer on Q11 and Q22.

knowledge_units.title: Ransomware restricts access to systems or data to extort payment
knowledge_units.claim: Ransomware is a type of malware that restricts access to a victim's system or data until a ransom is paid, commonly through cryptographic ransomware that encrypts files or locker ransomware that blocks system access.
knowledge_units.explanation: Oz et al. define ransomware as malware that prevents or limits users from accessing their system or data until a ransom is paid. They classify ransomware into cryptographic ransomware, which encrypts victim files and demands payment for decryption, and locker ransomware, which prevents access to a system without necessarily encrypting data. They also describe ransomware attacks as involving phases such as infection, possible command-and-control communication, destruction through encryption or locking, and extortion through ransom instructions.
recommended_actions: Treat ransomware as a malware threat that can affect access to systems or data.
Recognize that ransomware may encrypt files, lock systems, or combine extortion with data theft.
Be cautious with common ransomware infection vectors such as malicious emails, malicious links, drive-by downloads, malicious applications, and exploited vulnerabilities.
Use backups and timely security updates as part of ransomware risk reduction and recovery planning.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Ransomware is a subset of malware that prevents or limits users from accessing their system and/or data until a ransom is paid. The main objective of ransomware is extorting money from the victims.

Based on the employed methodology, ransomware is generally classified into two types. Cryptographic Ransomware: This variety of ransomware encrypts victim files, deletes or overwrites the original files, and demands a ransom payment for decryption of the files. Locker Ransomware: This type of ransomware prevents the victim from accessing its system by locking the screen or browser, and demands a ransom payment to unlock the system.  Unlike cryptographic ransomware, it does not encrypt the system or user data
Attack phases of ransomware can be summarized as follows: Infection... Communication with C&C servers... Destruction... Extortion.

Infection methods of ransomware can be categorized into five groups: malicious e-mails, SMS or instant messages (IMs), malicious applications, drive-by-download, and vulnerabilities. Malicious e-mails are the most commonly used infection vectors for ransomware. Attackers send spam e-mails to victims that have attachments containing ransomware [164]. Such spam campaigns can be distributed using botnets [110, 139]. Ransomware may come with an attached malicious file, or the e-mail may contain a malicious link that will trigger the installation of ransomware once visited (drive-by download). SMS Messages or IMs are used frequently for mobile ransomware. In such kinds of infections, attackers send SMS messages or IMs to the victims that will cause them to browse a malicious website to download ransomware to their platforms [140, 147]. Malicious Applications are used by ransomware attackers who develop and deploy mobile applications that contain ransomware camouflaged as a benign application [140, 147]. Drive-by download happens when a user unknowingly visits an infected website or clicks a malicious advertisement (i.e., malvertisement) and then the malware is downloaded and installed without the user’s knowledge [176]. Vulnerabilities in the victim platform such as vulnerabilities in operating systems [40], browsers [163], or software can be used by ransomware authors as infection vectors. Attackers can use helper applications, exploit kits, to exploit the known or zero-day vulnerabilities in target systems. Attackers can redirect victims to those kits via malvertisement and malicious links

knowledge_units.title: Ransomware defense depends on prevention, detection, and recovery planning
knowledge_units.claim: Ransomware defense should combine prevention, detection, and recovery planning, because ransomware can infect systems through multiple vectors, restrict access to files or systems, and require recovery mechanisms such as key recovery, hardware-based recovery, cloud backup recovery, or external-storage backups.
knowledge_units.explanation: Oz et al. organize ransomware defense research around analysis, detection, recovery, and other defense mechanisms. They describe ransomware recovery for PCs/workstations through three main categories: recovery of keys, recovery of files via hardware, and recovery of files via cloud backup. For mobile devices, they also mention recovery approaches involving flash-memory behavior analysis and periodic backups to external storage. Oz et al. further identify delayed upgrades or critical software patches and security-unaware end-users as major factors behind ransomware success. This supports treating backups, patching, awareness of infection vectors, and detection/recovery planning as complementary parts of ransomware risk reduction.
recommended_actions: Maintain backups that can support recovery if ransomware encrypts, destroys, or restricts access to files.
Do not delay upgrades or security-related software patches.
Train users to recognize ransomware infection vectors such as malicious emails, malicious links, malicious applications, drive-by downloads, and exploited vulnerabilities.
Treat ransomware defense as a combination of prevention, detection, and recovery rather than relying on one measure alone.
Consider recovery planning across relevant device types, including PCs/workstations and mobile devices.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Ransomware recovery research for PCs/workstations shows that recovery of the destruction performed by ransomware can be achieved in three different ways: recovery of keys, recovery of files via hardware, or recovery of files via cloud backup.
Recovery of Keys: Kolodenker et al. proposed PayBreak - a key-escrow mechanism that intends to capture encryption key(s) by hooking the cryptography APIs and decrypt the victim files.

Recovery of Files via Hardware: The studies presented in this category aim to recover encrypted files of victims by utilizing the characteristics of storage hardware (i.e., SSD).  Alternatively... Min et al. designed an SSD system that performs an automated backup and minimizes the backup space overhead. Their system utilizes a detection component that leverages hardware accelerator to detect the infected pages in the memory.

Recovery of Files via Cloud Backup: Some of the recovery mechanisms in the literature aimed to recover files utilizing cloud environment for backup purposes. Yun et al. proposed a backup system named CLDSafe that is deployed on the cloud.

Ransomware Recovery for Mobile Devices. Considering the recovery solutions for mobile devices to enable data recovery from ransomware attacks, there exist only two studies... a recovery-based ransomware defense strategy for mobile devices that are equipped with flash memory as external storage. It collects the access behaviors of ransomware samples and applies K-mean clustering to identify the unique access patterns to the Flash Transaction Layer. In [59] Yalew et al. aimed to recover from ransomware by periodically performing backups to an external storage.
Although upgrades and patches may aim to repair such vulnerabilities, it is vital not to delay upgrades or security-related software patches to prevent the infection.
Security training of end-users in terms of the infection vectors of ransomware is very vital.
